authorEyebrow: Inspired by Seth Gillihan's CBT Toolbox · The Think CBT Workbook

home.title: CBT
home.description: A focused CBT section built around thought records, distortion awareness, and quiet review.

program.startDescription: A guided journey through the CBT process - from assessment to lasting resilience - using the tools in this module. Go at your own pace.
program.graduationBody: You built real momentum. Here's what you did:
program.graduationBodyEmpty: You reached the end at your own pace. Your tools are here whenever you need them.
program.abandonDescription: This only stops the program tracking. Your saved thought records, activities, goals, and logs stay in place.

assessment.title: Understand where you are
assessment.description: Map the problem, set goals, clarify your values, and start noticing how you think, feel and act day to day.

formulation.title: See how the problem works
formulation.description: Trace the layers of thinking and beliefs that keep the problem going.

thinking.title: Work with your thinking
thinking.description: Catch and re-balance negative automatic thoughts with thought records.

behavioural.title: Change what you do
behavioural.description: Re-engage with meaningful activity (PACE) and face avoidance step by step.

resilience.title: Build lasting resilience
resilience.description: Steady your emotions with brief mindfulness, then consolidate everything into a personal resilience plan.

tasks.dailyNoticing: Notice your thoughts, feelings & behaviours today
tasks.resiliencePlan: Write your resilience plan and personal slogan

program.ready: You've finished this phase - ready for the next?
program.advanceEarlyConfirmBody: You haven't finished this phase's steps yet. Move on anyway?
program.noDailyPractice: No daily task this phase - take your time with the step above.

insights.title: Insights
insights.exerciseMood: Average mood on exercise days: {{withExercise}} vs {{withoutExercise}} without.
insights.exerciseMoodDetail: This compares mood logs on days with and without exercise.
insights.reviewBeliefDetail: Review dates and low alternative-belief strength can be useful checkpoints.
insights.recurringThoughtDetail: This exact thought appears {{count}} times. Consider whether a core belief sits underneath it.
insights.selfCareTrend: Self-care trend: movement on {{exerciseDays}}/{{totalDays}} recent days
insights.exposureProgressDetail: Completion here means an exposure item has at least one saved practice session.

pillars.introDescription: CBT works through three reinforcing pillars. Positive change in any one pillar makes the others easier.

think.title: Think
think.description: Examine the thoughts and beliefs under your reactions. Identify patterns, test them, and form more accurate views.

act.title: Act
act.description: Action precedes motivation. Schedule meaningful behavior, expose yourself gradually to what you avoid, and watch mood follow.

be.title: Be
be.description: Present-moment awareness without judgment. Use the shared meditation and mood tools - and the self-care log to protect baseline mood.

strategyDescriptions.distortions: Reference for the core thinking patterns.
strategyDescriptions.activities: Pleasure and mastery, on the calendar.
strategyDescriptions.selfCare: Daily check on sleep, body, connection.
strategyDescriptions.recovery: The keys you keep coming back to.

weeklyReview.title: Weekly review
weeklyReview.description: A quiet look at the past seven days - mood, activity, goals, and what to carry forward.
weeklyReview.moodTrendDescription: Average score per day over the past seven days.
weeklyReview.noMoodData: No mood logs for this week yet. Log your mood from the dashboard to see it here.
weeklyReview.goalProgressDescription: Milestone completion for your active goals.

reflection.title: Reflection prompt
reflection.description: One question to sit with before the next week begins.
reflection.prompt1: What is one thing that went better than expected this week?
reflection.prompt2: Where did you notice yourself being hardest on yourself? What would you say to a friend in the same situation?
reflection.prompt3: Which activity gave you the most energy or sense of accomplishment?
reflection.prompt4: What is one small thing you would like to do differently next week?

beliefs.title: Core beliefs
beliefs.description: Surface the deeper beliefs that shape your reactions, and gently test their grip.
beliefs.emptyDescription: When you notice a thought theme returning, name the belief beneath it here.
beliefs.newDescription: Capture the belief, the situations that trigger it, and a more balanced alternative.
beliefs.editDescription: Update the belief, evidence, or strength ratings.
beliefs.beliefStatementHint: A short sentence that captures the belief in your own words.
beliefs.triggeringSituationsHint: List moments where this belief tends to show up.
beliefs.evidenceForHint: What experiences seem to back this belief?
beliefs.evidenceAgainstHint: What experiences or facts contradict it?
beliefs.alternativeBeliefPlaceholder: Example: I am still learning and that's enough.
beliefs.originalStrength: How strongly the original belief feels (0–100)
beliefs.originalStrengthHint: 0 means it has no grip; 100 means it feels completely true.
beliefs.alternativeStrengthHint: Even small belief in the alternative is a starting point.
beliefs.reinforcementPlanHint: What small actions or reminders will strengthen the alternative belief?
beliefs.reinforcementPlanPlaceholder: Example: Re-read this entry when I notice the old belief.
beliefs.strengthTrackerDescription: Update these ratings whenever you revisit the belief.
beliefs.deleteMessage: This permanently removes the belief and its strength history. This can't be undone.

exposure.title: Anxiety & exposure
exposure.description: Build a step-by-step plan to face anxiety, one manageable step at a time.
exposure.emptyDescription: Build your first exposure hierarchy when you're ready to start.
exposure.newDescription: Name the anxiety, then list steps from easier to harder.
exposure.anxietyTypeHint: Helps you find the hierarchy later. Free-text.
exposure.itemsHint: Add at least one step. Rate each by anticipated anxiety (SUDS, 0–100).
exposure.worryLinkDescription: Work through worries by writing them down and testing their grip.

session.title: Exposure session
session.preSudsHint: How anxious you felt before starting.
session.safetyBehaviorPlaceholder: Example: Stayed near the exit, looked at my phone.
session.notesPlaceholder: Anything worth remembering for next time.

exposure.deleteMessage: This permanently removes the hierarchy and all of its items and session history. This can't be undone.

worry.title: Worry journal
worry.description: Sort worries into hypothetical or real problems. Each gets its own response.
worry.empty: No worries logged on this day
worry.emptyDescription: Try another date, or add an entry when a worry starts circling - getting it on paper often helps.
worry.newDescription: Capture the worry, then choose how to respond to it.
worry.statementPlaceholder: Write the worry as you hear it in your head.
worry.categoryLabel: What kind of worry is this?
worry.categoryHint: Hypothetical worries are about things that haven't happened. Real problems can be acted on now.
worry.probabilityHint: How likely does this actually feel, on reflection?
worry.evidenceForHint: What makes this worry feel real?
worry.evidenceAgainstHint: What suggests it might be unlikely or manageable?
worry.copingStatementHint: A reminder you can return to if the worry comes back.
worry.copingStatementPlaceholder: Example: Even if it happens, I'll handle it one step at a time.
worry.actionStepsHint: Concrete steps you can take. Add at least one.
worry.deleteMessage: This permanently removes the entry. This can't be undone.

tasks.title: Procrastination
tasks.description: Break stuck tasks into small steps and challenge the thoughts that hold them back.
tasks.emptyDescription: Add a task you've been avoiding to break it into manageable steps.
tasks.newDescription: Walk through one task in five small steps.
tasks.taskDescriptionHint: Be specific. A clear definition makes it easier to start.
tasks.avoidanceReason: Why have you been avoiding it? (optional)
tasks.avoidanceReasonPlaceholder: Example: I don't know where to start.
tasks.fearThoughtHint: The thought that makes it feel hard.
tasks.fearThoughtPlaceholder: Example: I'll do a bad job and people will see.
tasks.challengedThoughtHint: How would you respond to that fear, kindly?
tasks.challengedThoughtPlaceholder: Example: A draft doesn't need to be perfect. I can improve it after.
tasks.smallestStepDescription: The goal is to begin, not to finish perfectly. Write the first action so small that it feels doable even with low motivation.
tasks.stepsHint: Small steps. Aim for first ones you can finish in under 30 minutes.
tasks.stepDescriptionPlaceholder: What is the first concrete action?
tasks.rewardHint: Something to look forward to when you finish.
tasks.allDoneDescription: Mark the task complete to celebrate progress.

anger.title: Anger management
anger.description: Log anger episodes to spot patterns and try out different responses.
anger.empty: No anger logs on this day
anger.emptyDescription: Try another date, or log an episode to start spotting patterns.
anger.newDescription: Walk through the cycle: trigger, interpretation, urge, behavior, consequence.
anger.psychoeducationDescription: Anger usually follows a cycle: a trigger, a quick interpretation, an arousal spike, and an urge. Slowing down to log the cycle gives you a chance to choose your response instead of reacting.
anger.patternInsightDescription: Across your last {{count}} entries, average arousal was {{average}}/10.
anger.triggerPlaceholder: Example: My partner left dishes in the sink again.
anger.interpretation: What did you tell yourself about it?
anger.interpretationHint: The interpretation that fueled the anger.
anger.interpretationPlaceholder: Example: They don't respect my time.
anger.arousalLevelHint: How intense did the anger feel?
anger.urge: What did you feel an urge to do?
anger.behaviorChosenHint: Honest is more useful than ideal.
anger.behaviorChosenPlaceholder: Example: I went quiet for an hour.
anger.consequencePlaceholder: Example: We didn't talk for the rest of the evening.
anger.timeOutTaken: I took a time-out before responding
anger.alternativeInterpretationHint: Looking back, what other interpretation might fit?
anger.alternativeInterpretationPlaceholder: Example: They were exhausted and forgot.
anger.outcomeRatingHint: 1 = poor outcome, 10 = the response I wanted.
anger.editDescription: Update the details of this episode.
anger.deleteMessage: This permanently removes the log. This can't be undone.

selfCare.title: Self-care today
selfCare.description: A quick check-in for {{date}}. Save now and come back tomorrow.
selfCare.sleepLinkDesc: Log sleep in the Sleep tool.
selfCare.gratitudeLinkDesc: Capture gratitude in the Gratitude tool.
selfCare.mealsStructuredHint: How structured were your meals today?

recovery.title: Recovery plan
recovery.description: Collect what helps, how your strategies connect, and what you want to return to during harder moments.

stats.title: Personal stats
stats.description: A simple snapshot of the CBT work you have recorded so far.

timeline.title: Journey timeline
timeline.description: When each part of your CBT toolkit first showed up.
timeline.empty: Your timeline will appear after you save records across the CBT tools.

export.title: Export recovery plan
export.description: Download or share a readable copy of this plan, including your stats and timeline.
export.exportError: Unable to export the recovery plan.

recovery.recoveryKeysHint: Short reminders of the actions, mindsets, or supports that work for you.
recovery.keyPlaceholder: Example: Take a walk before deciding what the day means.
recovery.personalSloganHint: A short phrase that captures the way you want to keep practicing.
recovery.strategyNotesHint: Write how each active strategy connects with the rest of your toolkit.
recovery.strategyNotePlaceholder: How does {{strategy}} connect with your other tools?
recovery.challengePlansHint: Name likely future challenges and the steps you want ready before they show up.
recovery.challengeDescriptionPlaceholder: Example: A stressful week at work makes me withdraw.
recovery.copingStepPlaceholder: Example: Text one trusted person before isolating.
recovery.maintenanceCommitmentsHint: Small practices you want to keep, without pressure or punishment.
recovery.commitmentPlaceholder: Example: Do a weekly review on Sunday.
recovery.saveError: Unable to save the recovery plan.

history.title: Thought history
history.description: Saved CBT records stay private, editable, and easy to revisit.
history.emptyDescription: Try another date, or add a record from the CBT section - it appears here once saved.

learn.title: Distortion guide
learn.description: Short explanations of the common thinking patterns to watch for.
learn.useGentlyDescription: These patterns are prompts for reflection, not labels to pin on yourself.

intro.title: The CBT Toolkit: Think, Act, and Be Your Way to Better Mental Health
intro.subtitle: CBT is a solution-focused therapy based on the connection between thoughts, feelings and behaviours. This section breaks down the core Think, Act, Be pillars and the essential self-care habits required to retrain the brain for lasting emotional well-being.
intro.actBody: Gradually re-engage in rewarding activities to build 'pleasure and mastery,' even when motivation is low.
intro.thinkBody: Identify negative automatic thoughts and test them against objective facts to find more accurate perspectives.
intro.beBody: Cultivate non-judgmental awareness of the present moment to disengage from rumination and future-oriented worries.
intro.beliefsBody: Uncover the deep-rooted mental 'maps' that act as filters, often causing us to ignore positive data.
intro.exposureBody: Systematically face fears to teach the brain that the perceived danger is exaggerated and manageable.
intro.sleepBody: Prioritise restorative sleep to regulate the limbic system and improve daytime emotional resilience.
intro.tableRow2Focus: Controlling breath and facing physical symptoms
intro.attribution: This section is inspired by Cognitive Behavioral Therapy Made Simple by Seth J. Gillihan PhD.

goals.title: Goals
goals.description: Set meaningful goals and track them with milestones.
goals.emptyDescription: Set your first goal to get started. Break it into milestones so progress feels real.
goals.newDescription: Define a goal and the steps that will get you there.
goals.editDescription: Update the goal or its milestones.
goals.lifeDomainHint: Which area of life does this goal belong to?
goals.goalTypeHint: What kind of change are you working towards?
goals.titlePlaceholder: Describe the goal in a few words.
goals.descriptionPlaceholder: Add context or motivation if it helps.
goals.targetDateHint: Pick a date you're aiming for, if it helps.
goals.milestonesHint: Break the goal into concrete steps. Add at least one.

confirm.completedDescription: This will move the goal to your past goals.

activities.title: Activities
activities.description: Schedule and track activities that bring pleasure or a sense of mastery.
activities.emptyDescription: Schedule an activity to get started. Even small steps count.
activities.newDescription: Plan an activity and optionally rate your mood before.
activities.categoryHint: Is this about enjoyment or building skill?
activities.paceCategoryHint: Optional: categorise this activity to help balance your week.
activities.moodBeforeHint: How are you feeling before you start?
activities.valueLinkedDescription: You came from {{domain}} values. Choose one small activity that would move this area by one step.

values.title: Values clarification
values.description: Rate the values that matter to you, then choose the few that will be most instrumental in reaching your goals.
values.tierLabel: How important is this to you?
values.prioritiesHint: Pick up to 6 Highly Important values, in priority order.
values.prioritiesEmpty: Mark values Highly Important below to add them here.
values.priorityFull: You've reached the maximum of 6 priorities.
values.priorityRequired: Add at least one priority value to save.

accepting.label: Accepting
accepting.description: Open to and willing to experience thoughts and feelings without resistance or judgement.

affectionate.label: Affectionate
affectionate.description: Displaying and expressing love or strong feelings for others.

aspiring.label: Aspiring
aspiring.description: Actively seeking opportunities and striving for success.

assertive.label: Assertive
assertive.description: Respectfully standing up for my rights and balancing my needs with the needs of others.

authentic.label: Authentic
authentic.description: Being genuine, honest and true to myself.

caring.label: Caring
caring.description: Helpful and considerate to myself and others.

challenging.label: Challenging
challenging.description: Willing to examine and challenge unhelpful or unworkable patterns of behaviour.

compassionate.label: Compassionate
compassionate.description: Recognising and acting to alleviate suffering for myself and others.

conforming.label: Conforming
conforming.description: Respectful and obedient of helpful rules and obligations.

cooperating.label: Cooperating
cooperating.description: Working collaboratively and harmoniously with others.

courageous.label: Courageous
courageous.description: Brave and persistent in the face of fear, threat or difficulty.

curious.label: Curious
curious.description: Open-minded, exploratory and interested in new and alternative approaches.

emotionally-aware.label: Emotionally aware
emotionally-aware.description: Receptive to my own feelings and the feelings of others.

encouraging.label: Encouraging
encouraging.description: Promoting and rewarding behaviour that I value in myself and others.

equality.label: Equality
equality.description: Treating others fairly, consistently and equally.

fitness.label: Fitness
fitness.description: Maintaining or improving my physical, psychological and emotional wellbeing.

flexible.label: Flexible
flexible.description: Adjusting and adapting readily to changing circumstances.

forgiving.label: Forgiving
forgiving.description: Coming to terms with or letting go of negative feelings towards myself and others.

generosity.label: Generosity
generosity.description: Sharing and offering time, attention and resources to myself and others.

gratitude.label: Gratitude
gratitude.description: Appreciative and valuing of the things that I have in my life.

honesty.label: Honesty
honesty.description: Truthful and sincere with myself and others.

humility.label: Humility
humility.description: Modest about my contributions whilst allowing my achievements to speak for themselves.

humour.label: Humour
humour.description: Seeking out and engaging in the humorous side of life; not taking myself too seriously.

independent.label: Independent
independent.description: Self-directive, contained and able to support myself.

industrious.label: Industrious
industrious.description: Hard-working and productive with my time and resources.

open-minded.label: Open-minded
open-minded.description: Approaching issues from an objective position, listening and considering other views.

orderly.label: Orderly
orderly.description: Planning, prioritising and being organized in my life.

patient.label: Patient
patient.description: Waiting calmly and in line for what I want or need.

respectful.label: Respectful
respectful.description: Polite, considerate and showing positive regard to others.

safe.label: Safe
safe.description: Acting in a secure, protective and stable manner.

self-aware.label: Self-aware
self-aware.description: Aware of my own thoughts, feelings and actions, considering how I come across to others.

self-disciplined.label: Self-disciplined
self-disciplined.description: Acting according to my values and commitments, over my mood or vulnerabilities.

tenacious.label: Tenacious
tenacious.description: Persisting despite problems and difficulties and without giving up.

tolerant.label: Tolerant
tolerant.description: Coming to terms with, acknowledging and respecting things that clash with my own opinions.

trusted.label: Trusted
trusted.description: Discrete, loyal, faithful, sincere and reliable.

mood.description: A quick check on how you are feeling right now.
mood.editDescription: Adjust the score, emotions, date, or notes.
mood.situationPlaceholder: What was happening? e.g. an email from my boss
mood.behavioursPlaceholder: What did you do (or avoid doing)?
mood.scoreHint: 1 is very low, 5 is very good.
mood.notesPlaceholder: Anything you want to capture about this moment.
mood.notFound: We couldn't find that mood entry.
mood.responseHelp: What did you do, or avoid?

record.newDescription: Use any prompts that help. Blank fields can be saved and completed later.
record.editDescription: Edit a saved record without losing its history.
record.situationHint: What happened, where were you, and what set the moment off?
record.situationPlaceholder: Keep it concrete. A few sentences is enough.
record.situationExample: Example: I saw an email from my manager and my chest tightened immediately.
record.natsHint: What thoughts went through your mind? Add each one and rate how strongly you believed it.
record.beliefRatingHint: How strongly did you believe this thought? (0 = not at all, 100 = completely)
record.hotThoughtInstruction: Which of these thoughts caused you the most distress?
record.natsRequired: Add at least one thought to continue.
record.balancedThoughtHint: Write the calmer, more balanced response you want to keep.
record.emotionsLabelHint: Pick the feelings that fit best.
record.intensityBeforeHint: Optional. 0 means no intensity; 100 means as strong as it gets.
record.patternsChooseHint: Optional: choose the thinking patterns that seem closest. You can pick more than one, or skip this step.
record.evidenceForHint: Optional. Add one fact per line.
record.evidenceForPlaceholder: Example: The message says we need to talk.
record.evidenceAgainstHint: Optional. Add one fact per line.
record.evidenceAgainstPlaceholder: Example: My manager has sent routine check-in messages before.
record.balancedThoughtPlaceholder: Aim for something believable and kinder, not forced positivity.
record.balancedThoughtExample: Example: I do not know what the email means yet. One message is not proof that I failed.
record.intensityAfterHint: Optional. Rate the same emotion after writing the balanced thought.
record.outcomeNotesHint: Optional. Capture what changed, what helped, or what you want to remember.
record.outcomeNotesPlaceholder: Example: The thought still feels uncomfortable, but less certain.

detail.notFoundDescription: The record may have been archived or removed.
detail.title: Thought record
detail.expandedDescription: This record includes optional evidence or intensity notes.

all-or-nothing.title: All-or-nothing thinking
all-or-nothing.shortDescription: Seeing the situation in extremes instead of noticing the middle ground.
all-or-nothing.reflectionPrompt: What would a more balanced version of this situation sound like?

catastrophizing.title: Catastrophizing
catastrophizing.shortDescription: Jumping quickly to the worst-case outcome and treating it as likely.
catastrophizing.reflectionPrompt: What is difficult here, and what is only a fear about the future?

mind-reading.title: Mind reading
mind-reading.shortDescription: Assuming you know what other people think without enough evidence.
mind-reading.reflectionPrompt: What facts do I have, and what am I filling in myself?

fortune-telling.title: Fortune telling
fortune-telling.shortDescription: Treating a prediction as if it is already proven.
fortune-telling.reflectionPrompt: What other outcome is also plausible right now?

overgeneralization.title: Overgeneralization
overgeneralization.shortDescription: Using one event as proof that the same pattern will always repeat.
overgeneralization.reflectionPrompt: Is this one moment, or is it truly evidence for every situation?

should-statements.title: Should statements
should-statements.shortDescription: Using rigid rules that leave little room for context or humanity.
should-statements.reflectionPrompt: What would become kinder and more realistic if I changed should to prefer?

emotional-reasoning.title: Emotional reasoning
emotional-reasoning.shortDescription: Treating a feeling as proof that a conclusion must be true.
emotional-reasoning.reflectionPrompt: What do I feel, and what do I actually know?

discounting-the-positive.title: Discounting the positive
discounting-the-positive.shortDescription: Dismissing helpful evidence because it feels too small to count.
discounting-the-positive.reflectionPrompt: What positive detail am I brushing aside too quickly?

labeling.title: Labeling
labeling.shortDescription: Reducing yourself or someone else to a harsh global label.
labeling.reflectionPrompt: How can I describe the moment without turning it into an identity?

personalization.title: Personalization
personalization.shortDescription: Taking on more blame or responsibility than the facts justify.
personalization.reflectionPrompt: What part is mine, and what part is not actually under my control?

blaming.title: Blaming
blaming.shortDescription: Holding others responsible for your pain, or taking the blame for everything yourself.
blaming.reflectionPrompt: What part of this situation is mine, and what part belongs to someone else?

perfectionistic-thinking.title: Perfectionistic thinking
perfectionistic-thinking.shortDescription: Setting standards so high that anything short of perfect counts as failure.
perfectionistic-thinking.reflectionPrompt: What would 'good enough' look like here, and is that actually acceptable?

comparing.title: Comparing
comparing.shortDescription: Measuring yourself against others and deciding you come up short.
comparing.reflectionPrompt: Am I comparing my inside to someone else's outside?

change-fallacy.title: Change fallacy
change-fallacy.shortDescription: Believing others must change before you can feel better.
change-fallacy.reflectionPrompt: What can I do that does not depend on someone else changing first?

control-fallacy.title: Control fallacy
control-fallacy.shortDescription: Either feeling fully controlled by external forces, or responsible for controlling everything.
control-fallacy.reflectionPrompt: What is actually within my control, and what do I need to let go of?

fairness-fallacy.title: Fairness fallacy
fairness-fallacy.shortDescription: Feeling resentful when life or other people don't match your sense of what is fair.
fairness-fallacy.reflectionPrompt: Is fairness the right lens here, or am I making a demand the world can't always meet?

reward-fallacy.title: Reward fallacy
reward-fallacy.shortDescription: Expecting that sacrifice or hard work will always pay off, and feeling bitter when it doesn't.
reward-fallacy.reflectionPrompt: Was I doing this for the outcome, or for the value of the action itself?

grounding.title: Grounding
grounding.description: Quick techniques to anchor yourself in the present moment when anxiety or distress spikes.
grounding.authorEyebrow: Inspired by DBT distress tolerance · Marsha Linehan
grounding.doneMessage: You got through it. Take a breath.

54321.shortDescription: Anchor yourself in the present through all five senses.
steps: Name 5 things you can see right now.
Notice 4 things you can physically feel - clothes on skin, feet on floor.
Listen for 3 things you can hear.
Find 2 things you can smell - or recall a familiar scent.

cold-water.title: Cold water
cold-water.shortDescription: A sharp physical sensation that interrupts the stress response.
steps: Go to a tap or fill a bowl with cold water.
Submerge your wrists, or splash your face.
Hold for 30 seconds. Notice the temperature, the sensation, the boundary between cold and skin.
Dry off slowly. Notice how your body feels now.

feet-floor.title: Feet on the floor
feet-floor.shortDescription: Use physical contact with the ground to settle anxiety.
steps: Sit or stand with both feet flat on the floor.
Press your feet down firmly. Notice the pressure and texture.
Take three slow breaths while keeping your feet grounded.
Stamp your feet three times and notice the vibration.

welcome.title: Anchor yourself
welcome.subtitle: Quick techniques to interrupt anxiety and return to the present moment.

when.title: When to use grounding
when.body: Anxiety spirals, overwhelm, racing thoughts, dissociation, or any moment when the present feels hard to reach. Grounding is not a cure - it's a circuit breaker.

how.title: How it works
how.body: Grounding exercises engage the senses and the body, pulling attention out of anxious thought loops and back into physical reality. The nervous system can't be fully activated and fully grounded at the same time.

techniques.title: Available techniques
techniques.senseBody: Name five things you see, four you feel, three you hear, two you smell, one you taste.
techniques.breathBody: Inhale 4 counts, hold 4, exhale 4, hold 4 - repeat until calm.
techniques.coldBody: Wrists or face in cold water for 30 seconds interrupts the stress response.
techniques.feetBody: Press both feet down firmly, breathe slowly, notice the ground.

breathing.title: Breathing exercises
breathing.tagline: Short guided patterns to calm your nervous system right now.
breathing.startSessionHint: Opens your last pattern - switch any time.
breathing.description: Short guided patterns to calm your nervous system right now.
breathing.nudgeDescription: A short breathing exercise can help settle your nervous system.

builder.activePhaseRequired: Add at least an inhale or an exhale.
builder.saveError: Could not save. Please try again.

box-breathing.title: Box breathing
box-breathing.shortDescription: Equal counts of inhale, hold, exhale, hold - used to quickly reduce acute stress.
box-breathing.benefit: Activates the parasympathetic nervous system within a few cycles.

4-7-8.title: 4-7-8 breathing
4-7-8.shortDescription: A longer exhale that down-regulates the nervous system quickly.
4-7-8.benefit: The extended hold and slow exhale trigger the relaxation response.

coherent-breathing.title: Coherent breathing
coherent-breathing.shortDescription: Slow, equal inhale and exhale at about 5 breaths per minute.
coherent-breathing.benefit: Maximises heart rate variability and reduces anxiety over time.
